method_atom: Analysis of a first-order (discontinuous) transition in a disordered mean-field system with an exponentially large number of uncorrelated, randomly-valued configurations, driven by a tunable quantum perturbation that induces tunneling between configurations. At the transition the lowest eigenstate abruptly projects onto the unperturbed extremal configuration and the spectral gap between the two lowest levels becomes exponentially small in the size of the system. A two-time instanton (saddle-point) technique is introduced to estimate this vanishing gap, with direct consequences for adiabatic optimization schedules that must traverse such a transition.
object_atom: Mean-field spin glasses, concretely the quantum Random Energy Model and the broader class of 'Random First Order' models (e.g. random satisfiability benchmarks), studied as targets for quantum annealing.
brief_reason: The transferable part is the machinery of an exponentially-small gap at a first-order transition in a random mean-field landscape and its instanton estimation; the domain-specific part is that this landscape is a quantum spin-glass / random-satisfiability problem solved by annealing.
